mnemonics.title: C.D.C.O.P. - Constructor/Destructor Order Principle
mnemonics.content: Base constructors run BEFORE derived constructors; Derived destructors run BEFORE base destructors. Think: 'Parents before Children, Children before Parents' - Constructors: Parents→Children, Destructors: Children→Parents

mnemonics.title: 3-5-0 Rule Song
mnemonics.content: Need a destructor? Then you need copy constructor, copy assignment operator (that's 3). Add move operations for 5. Zero means let compiler handle all. Sing to 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star'

mnemonics.title: P.I.G. - Parameterized, Init, Global
mnemonics.content: Constructor types: P = Parameterized (takes arguments), I = Init (initialization list), G = Global (called automatically for global objects). Remember: 'PIG goes to market'

mnemonics.title: DESTROY - Destructor Execution Sequence
mnemonics.content: DESTRUCTOR sequence: 1. Destructor body executes 2. Members destroyed in reverse order 3. Base class destructor called. Mnemonic: 'Destroy Enemies Swiftly: Turn Order Reverses' - Execute, Reverse, Base

acronyms.usage: Used when creating a new object as a copy of an existing object. Called when passing by value or returning by value.
Used when assigning to an existing object from another object. Distinguished from copy constructor by operating on already-constructed object.
Used to transfer resources from a temporary (rvalue) object. Marked with && parameter. More efficient than copy for heavy resources.
Special member function with same name as class, no return type. Initializes objects. Called automatically when object is created.
Special member function with ~ prefix. Releases resources, closes files/connections. Called automatically when object goes out of scope.
C++ idiom where resources are acquired in constructor and released in destructor. Ensures automatic cleanup when object lifetime ends.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a family tree growing DOWNWARD. Base class at top, derived below. When baby (derived object) is born, grandparents (base) are set up first, then parents, then baby. When object dies (destructor), baby disappears first, then parents, then grandparents.

Think of it like a 'quick checkout counter' - items are initialized BEFORE entering the main store (constructor body). The initialization list is the express lane where variables get their values immediately.

Picture a remote control with batteries (base pointer). When you press delete, you want the actual toy (derived object) to be destroyed, not just the remote. Virtual makes it call the correct destructor.
Copy = photocopying a document (two originals exist). Move = cutting and pasting the file (one original, now in new location). Move transfers ownership without copying.
Imagine a balloon (object). Constructor inflates it (allocates memory). Destructor pops it (releases memory). When the balloon string is let go (scope ends), it pops automatically.

analogies.analogy: Building a house: Before you can live in a house, you must build it first with rooms, doors, and utilities. Constructor builds/initializes the object so it's ready to use.
Locking up a house: When you leave, you lock doors, turn off lights, ensure everything is secure. Destructor cleans up and releases resources when object is no longer needed.
Getting a duplicate key made: You give the locksmith your original key, they create an exact copy. Both keys open the same lock. Two objects share same data but are separate entities.
Moving to a new apartment: Instead of copying all furniture, you physically move everything from old to new place. The old place becomes empty. More efficient than copying everything.

Filling a form: Some fields MUST be filled at the top (initialization list) before you can proceed. Trying to assign values inside the form (constructor body) is slower and sometimes impossible (for const/references).
TV remote with universal code: Without virtual, pressing 'off' only turns off the remote (base pointer). With virtual, it turns off the actual TV (derived object) the remote is controlling.
Buying a pre-furnished apartment: Everything comes with standard, empty default values. You get what the builder (compiler) provides unless you customize.
Ordering a custom pizza: You specify toppings (parameters) and get exactly what you asked for. Different parameters = different customized objects.
